content: The upper end of a wire of diameter $$24 \mathrm{~mm}$$ and length $$1 \mathrm{~m}$$ is damped and its other end is twisted through an angle is $$30^{\circ}$$. The angle of shear is

The elastic limit of $$1 \mathrm{~kg}$$ mass is $$3.5 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$$. Find the maximum load that can be applied to a brass wire of a $$1 \mathrm{~mm}$$ diameter without exceeding the elastic unit.
explanation:
Stress $$=F / A$$

For elastic limit, stress $$=3.5 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$$ (given).

Thus, $$F=\left(\pi r^2\right)$$ stress

$$\begin{aligned} & W=3.14\left(\frac{1.0}{2} \times 10^{-3}\right)^2 \times 3.5 \times 10^{10} \\ & F=3.14 \times \frac{1^2}{4} \times 3.5 \times 10^4=2.75 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}$$

content: A wire of cross-section $$A$$ is stretched horizontally between two clamps located $$2 l$$ metre apart. A weight $$w \mathrm{~kg}$$ is suspended from the mid-point of the wire. If the mid-point sags vertically through a distance $$x<1$$, the strain produced is
explanation:
Here change in length is

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta & =[A C+B C]-2 l \\ & =2\left(l^2+x^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}-2 l \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =2 l\left(1+\frac{x^2}{l^2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}-2 l \\ & =2 l\left(1+\frac{1}{2} \frac{x^2}{l^2}\right)-2 l=\frac{x^2}{l} \\ \therefore \quad \text { Strain } & =\frac{\Delta l}{2 l}=\frac{x^2}{2 l^2} \end{aligned}$$

content: The Young's modulus of a rope of $$10 \mathrm{~m}$$ length and having diameter of $$2 \mathrm{~cm}$$ is $$200 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~dyne} / \mathrm{cm}^2$$. If the elongation produced in the rope is $$1 \mathrm{~cm}$$, the force applied on the rope is
explanation:
Young's modulus of a rope $$Y=\frac{F L}{A \Delta l}$$

Given,

$$\begin{aligned} & n=10 \mathrm{~m}, A=\pi r^2=\pi(1)^2=\pi ; \\ & Y=20 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{dyne} / \mathrm{cm}^2, \Delta l=1 \mathrm{~cm}, \\ & F=\frac{Y \cdot A \cdot \Delta l}{L} \\ & F=\frac{20 \times 10^{11} \times 1 \times 1}{10 \times 10^2} \\ & F=6.28 \times 10^9 \text { dyne } \\ & F=6.28 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}$$
